name: Urea (46% N)
precautions: Do not apply on wet foliage — causes leaf burn
Apply in moist soil and cover immediately to prevent volatilization
Do not apply near seed — causes germination injury
Split application reduces nitrogen losses by 30-40%
best_time: Basal at sowing, 1st top dressing at 25-30 DAS, 2nd at 45-50 DAS

name: DAP (Diammonium Phosphate)
application_method: Basal application at sowing; place below seed
precautions: Apply as basal dose only — phosphorus is immobile in soil
Place 5-7cm below and to the side of seed row
Excess use leads to zinc/iron deficiency — add micronutrients
Avoid surface broadcasting — phosphorus fixation in calcareous soils

name: MOP (Muriate of Potash)
application_method: Basal application; split in high-demand crops
precautions: Avoid in chloride-sensitive crops (tobacco, grapes, citrus) — use SOP instead
High chloride can reduce quality in fruits and potatoes
Apply in split doses for sandy soils (prone to leaching)
best_time: Basal at sowing; for sugarcane: 50% basal + 50% at earthing up

name: NPK Complex (10:26:26)
precautions: Provides balanced nutrition — reduces need for separate fertilizers
Still need additional urea for nitrogen top dressing

name: SSP (Single Super Phosphate)
precautions: Excellent for sulphur-loving crops (oilseeds, onion)
Cheaper source of phosphorus than DAP
Also provides calcium — good for acidic soils
best_time: At sowing; mix with soil before planting

name: Zinc Sulphate (ZnSO4 33%)
application_method: Soil application (10-25 kg/ha) or foliar spray (0.5%)
precautions: Most deficient micronutrient in Indian soils (48% soils zinc-deficient)
Critical for rice — zinc deficiency causes 'khaira' disease
Foliar spray: dissolve in warm water, add lime to neutralize acidity
Do not mix with DAP/phosphatic fertilizers — zinc fixation occurs
best_time: Soil application before sowing; foliar spray at 30 and 60 DAS

name: Vermicompost
application_method: Soil application at 2-4 tonnes per acre
subsidy: Subsidized under organic farming schemes (PKVY, MOVCDNER)
precautions: Apply 2-3 weeks before sowing for nutrient release
Improves soil structure, water retention, and microbial activity
Supplement with bio-fertilizers (Rhizobium, PSB, Azotobacter) for best results
Quality vermicompost should have C:N ratio < 20:1
best_time: 2-3 weeks before sowing, incorporate into soil

name: Neem Coated Urea
application_method: Same as urea; slow release reduces need for split application
subsidy: Same as urea (100% production is neem-coated in India)
precautions: Reduces nitrogen losses by 10-15% through nitrification inhibition
Acts as pest deterrent for soil insects (termites, nematodes)
Can be used for all crops where urea is recommended
Slower nutrient release — better efficiency in sandy soils
